As can be seen here clearly, the only data points that differ here are the p values for these 24 patients.

Taken these results at their face value, I have some simple questions:

Firstly, 67% reduction of 0.72 does not appear to be 0.00 from a simple math perspective. Was this a unique (to OI trial) calculation?

Secondly, even if the above is correct, how does the same 0.00 (n=24) annualized fracture rate or the same 67% reduction from 0.72 (n=24), grow in statistical-significance from month 6/9 to month 14/16?

All this is to say that I'm not sure how these "positive" Orbit p2 data (n=24) should be understood.

Regardless, it's important to remember that these n=24 Orbit p2 data has no control, either placebo or comparator. Therefore, it seems only reasonable, in my opinion, not to put undue weight in these data.

Discussion

In my opinion, the probability of either or both trials succeeding in their primary endpoint is low to none.

My reasons:

Firstly, there have been no prior successful placebo-controlled, or comparator-controlled annualized fracture rate reduction data to support the prospect of Orbit p3 or Cosmic p3 succeeding in meeting the primary endpoint.

Orbit and Cosmic are not designed to repeat the successful smaller phase 2 with the same-primary-endpoint in the same ITT (intend-to-treat) patients.

Instead, they are trying to meet a tough primary endpoint (which had never been studied), against controls, for the first time.

For Cosmic, it's the first time UX143 is being studied in OI children.

For example, if Orbit p3 (against placebo) at month 12 was as good as Orbit p2 (n=24) at month 9 (against pre-treatment), namely 67% reduction (p=0.042), it would have missed the pre-specified threshold of p <0.04.

Or as seen in ASTEROID adult p2b data, UX143's effect seems to worsen by month 18 (though these are not for the same endpoints used in Orbit).

Secondly, in my opinion, it's even harder for Cosmic to beat the IV-bisphosphonate control.

Per MREO's 2025 annual report:

There are no therapies approved by the FDA or EMA for the treatment of OI. The only treatments available to OI patients are the acute management of fractures as they occur and drugs such as bisphosphonates which are typically used to treat osteoporosis and are not approved for OI but are commonly used off-label in both children and adults. Bisphosphonates slow down the rate at which osteoclasts resorb bone. These anti-resorptives include Aredia (pamidronate), Fosamax (alendronate) and Reclast (zoledronic acid). Bisphosphonates have not consistently been shown to reduce fractures in pediatric or adult OI patients and the effect of long-term therapy with these drugs remains unclear in both children and adults.

For our purpose here, it's important to remember that these bisphosphonates, e.g. pamidronate, alendronate, and zoledronic acid have been reported to be beneficial in treating OI children e.g. "increases mobility and bone density, improves quality of life, and reduces family dependence in children with OI."

In other words, it's one (hard) thing to beat the placebo control, it's another (harder thing) to beat an active control that potentially has a real treatment effect in OI children.

Lastly, in my opinion, UX143's mechanism of action is indirect and does not address the underlying cause of OI.

In brief and simple terms, the underlying cause of most of OI (which has four main subtypes) is the "mutations in the COL1A1 or COL1A2 genes" which results in reduced or dysfunctional type I collagen. Type I collagen is deemed essential for healthy, strong bones.

UX143 does not address this root cause, instead, it inhibits a different naturally-occurring protein called "sclerostin" that regulates normal bone metabolism.

In my opinion, if sclerostin plays no role in causing the bone to become brittle in the first place (which is caused by reduced or dysfunctional type I collagen), inhibiting sclerostin alone, will likely do little towards treating OI effectively, similar to bisphosphonates that also do not address the root cause.

Financials

According to Ultragenyx's Q3 earning call, as of September 30, "cash, cash equivalents, and marketable debt securities stood at $447 million, with an additional $400 million from the Crysvita royalty transaction". The net loss in Q3 was $180.4M.

During the call, the company "reaffirm (their) path to full-year GAAP profitability in 2027.”

For Mereo BioPharma, as of Sept 30, 2025, their cash and cash equivalent is $48.7M and the net loss in Q3 is $7M, per their Q3 report.

Concluding Thoughts & Risks

For the reasons expressed earlier, I am bearish towards the outcomes of Ultragenyx and Mereo BioPharma's OI phase 3 trials (Orbit and Cosmic).

In my estimation, it's unlikely that either or both will successfully meet the primary endpoint, against the placebo or active comparator control.

In the event of one or both p3 OI trials failure to meet the primary endpoint, both stocks will likely drop (significantly if both trials fail), as for both companies, the OI market represents a major near-term market opportunity.

Any developmental or regulatory setback or obstacle will no doubt be met with a dramatic sell-off.

Risks of a bearish thesis include, but are not limited to: both p3 trials are successful, M&A activity towards either company, any other positive material news in either company that may or may not be related to Orbit & Cosmic data, FDA's apparent regulatory flexibility in the event of mixed results etc..

Thanks very much for reading. I hope that this article is clear and useful to your own research into these two companies.

All the best!

data.title: Micron: Here's Why The Stock Has Been Soaring (Hint: It's Not HBM) (Downgrade)
data.description:
Micron Technology, Inc. (MU) has given shareholders a lot to celebrate the last few months as shares have doubled since August as DRAM and NAND have enjoyed favorable pricing environments and increased demand. With earnings approaching next month, I think investors should remain bullish despite the run-up.

In my last piece on MU back in September, I discussed the company's Q4 earnings results and the ongoing memory "supercycle," where all of Micron's product lines are in favorable pricing environments simultaneously. I rated the stock a Strong Buy, as I have done multiple times for over a year now, and it has since gained an additional 30%. That article can be read here.

Micron's run has in part been fueled by the myriad AI deals that have been struck between OpenAI (OPENAI) and companies like Oracle (ORCL), Advanced Micro Devices (AMD), Amazon (AMZN), Broadcom (AVGO) and others for billions upon billions of dollars of compute. All of these data centers will need memory and lots of it, including NAND, DDR5, and, of course, high bandwidth memory, or HBM.

I think when many investors think of Micron's role in the AI arms race, they tend to focus on HBM as the primary driver because of its vital role in AI accelerators. Nvidia's Blackwell chips use 192 GB of HBM3E and its upcoming Blackwell Ultra processors will use 288 GB. Not to be outdone, AMD's MI355X uses 288 GB of HBM3E and its upcoming MI400 GPUs are expected to sport a whopping 432 GB of HBM4! I think it's apparent that the memory needs of these chips will only continue to accelerate and it's no surprise Micron is sold out of HBM capacity through the end of 2026.

However, HBM only accounted for around $2 billion of the company's $9 billion in DRAM revenue in Q4, or 22%, and only about 17% of total revenues. Are increased earnings potential expectations for HBM truly what's behind MU's meteoric rise?

If the title of this article didn't tip you off already, I think the answer is no. The much simpler answer is that DRAM and NAND prices have been going absolutely bonkers the last two months as demand has surged and supply shortages have become critical in a very short period of time. I'm not talking about a 30% increase in two months, I'm talking a 30% increase in one week.

Zooming out to the big picture, DRAM prices are up more than 170% compared to this time last year as AI spending pushes DDR5, which data centers require in substantial supply, to new heights and is even giving commodity DDR3 and DDR4 a significant boost:

It's typical for prices to rise coming out of a downcycle but this type of price surge is much more rare and indicates a broad spectrum imbalance between supply and demand, in this case to Micron's benefit. On the DRAM side, some of this shortage is likely directly caused by memory manufacturers focusing expenditures and production lines on making HBM in order to capture the high margins and establish share in the fledgling market.

What makes this a true supercycle for Micron is that NAND demand is also surging right after Micron, SK Hynix (OTCPK:HXSC.F), and Samsung (OTCPK:SSNLF) scaled back production in order to clear out oversupply that was dragging on results in Q2 and Q3 2025. This has, in turn, caused a comparable rise in NAND prices, which have doubled over the past 6 months and much of 2026 capacity appears to be sold out.

I have been banging the drum on MU for a while now, rating it a Strong Buy when it was trading for $88 back in September 2024, but even I didn't see this coming. As it stands, the company is well on its way to blowing past record revenue and earnings, and potentially even record margins as prices continue to skyrocket -- throw whatever estimates you had out the window, Micron could reach $16 or more in EPS in FY2026. That's the good news. Unfortunately, there is also bad news.

Higher memory prices usually cause two things:

  1. Memory manufacturers have a strong incentive to build more capacity to meet the increased demand lest their competitors do it first and take market share and profits. Supply increases.
  2. High prices scare off consumers completely and cause enterprise customers to scale back purchases. Demand decreases.

This is the equilibrium of the memory market and, no, this time will not be different. The Big 3 memory makers will likely be cautious, but supply will creep up to meet demand as all these companies want a piece of the capital expenditures that are fueling data center build-outs worldwide. As they do, prices will return to normal, and if a demand shock (like the bursting of the AI bubble) hits during this transition, we will likely see supply outstrip demand, leading to another downcycle. Even if that doesn't occur, equilibrium will return eventually. The question isn't "what" will happen, but "when."

Investor Takeaway

The stock's momentum has slowed a bit as the market seems to be getting a bit wary about the possibility that the AI bubble is finally close to bursting. Nvidia reported earnings that appear poised to keep the market happy for the moment (MU was up 4% after hours), but concerns remain.

As I have stated many times on these articles, MU tends to peak a quarter or two before revenue peaks and tends to rise before revenue troughs. So where's the peak?

Answering that will be a fairly tough ask and, personally, I have made my money in MU by buying at pronounced troughs and selling at pronounced peaks. I was of the opinion that anything below $100 per share was a steal considering the company's impending favorable pricing environment for NAND and DRAM (despite NAND oversupply hurting the stock in August) and the company's inroads into the HBM market. To me, that was a great example of a pronounced trough.

Pronounced peaks are harder to spot and act upon -- it's easier to ride the stock up from a downtrend than to know where to sell during an uptrend. From my experience in this market and the current capacity indications for 2026, I don't think that we are in the midst of a pronounced peak just yet. However, after a nearly 100% gain in 3 months, I think the risk-reward profile has certainly shifted towards the risk side. That said, I am not selling any shares just yet as I think FY2026 will see Micron earning boatloads of cash that will keep market sentiment bullish and generate upward momentum.

It's possible that the hyperscalers that are driving data center memory demand continue to buy DRAM and NAND even at elevated prices, which would launch MU even higher. I'd recommend investors keep a close eye on spot prices and management comments regarding capacity expansions and bit growth. As such, it's been a good run but I am downgrading MU from a Strong Buy to a Buy.

Thanks for reading!

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Gilead Sciences: Rating Upgrade Warranted On HIV Division Strength
data.description:
Investment Overview

The last time I posted on Gilead Sciences, Inc. (GILD), the California-based Pharma giant, and a company I am invested in, it was to cover its Q1 2025 earnings and business updates in April. In my conclusion, I wrote:

At present, I'd class Gilead as a "middle of the road" Pharma, in terms of dividend yield, price to sales - ~4.3x - and price to earnings - ~13x - and that means I am comfortable holding, for the time being, but may also consider exiting this holding and investing in a similar company with a deeper, richer, pipeline, or a superior dividend yield.

Spoiler alert: I am still long of Gilead. At the end of October, Gilead announced its Q3 earnings. The company reported product sales of $7.3bn. $5.3bn of those sales came from its HIV division, $819m from its liver disease portfolio, $432m from its two cell therapy assets, Yescarta and Tecartus, $357m from its oncology med Trodelvy, and $277m from its COVID therapy Veklury.

Overall revenues fell year-on-year, from $7.52bn in Q3 2024, which is mainly down to slowing sales of Veklury, which fell from $692m, to $277m in Q3 this year. Total revenues amounted to $7.77bn, however, up from $7.1bn, owing to "royalty, contract and other revenues" of $424m, with $400m attributed to a 2018 IP asset sale.

Non-GAAP operating expenses fell from $3.3bn in Q3 2024, to $2.86bn, down ~13%, and non-GAAP operating income was reported as $3.92bn - margin of 50%. Non-GAAP net income was recorded as $3.1bn, and non-GAAP diluted earnings per share ("EPS") $2.47, compared to $2.02 in Q3 2024. Overall, Gilead easily surpassed analyst's expectations.

2025 Guidance was presented as follows (slide taken from Gilead's Q3 earnings presentation):

As we can see, while revenue forecasts have been consistent throughout the year - give or take ~$400m of Veklury revenues and product sales - EPS has been nudged upwards by $0.5 over the course of the year - forward price to sales ("P/S") ratio for Gilead is ~5.5x, and forward price to earnings ("P/E") ratio ~15.5x. These ratios have risen since my April note - and that's because Gilead stock has defied my Hold call and risen in value since by nearly 25%.

Gilead: What Has Been Driving Impressive Share Price Gains? HIV, Not Oncology

On a five-year basis, Gilead shares are up ~115% - across that period, among U.S. "Big Pharma" companies, only Eli Lilly (LLY) - up >650%, and AbbVie (ABBV) - up >180%, shares have performed better.

In May last year, Gilead stock was worth ~$65 per share, so it has risen ~100% in 18 months, and the upside has been more or less constant. In my view, this may be due to the company learning the lessons from several misguided M&A deals that attempted to kick-start its oncology division, and the unstoppable growth of its HIV division.

Gilead's CEO, Daniel O'Day, joined the company in 2019, from his former role as CEO of Roche Pharmaceuticals, a company whose primary focus is on oncology. The new CEO was expected to bring a similar focus to Gilead - as I noted in my last post:

Gilead has thrown plenty of money at its oncology division - for example, the $21bn acquisition of Immunomedics, $4.9bn buyout of Forty Seven, and $12bn paid for Kite Pharma, yet in Q1 the division returned just $757m of revenues.

In Q3, that figure was $789m. The Immunomedics acquisition gave Gilead access to Trodelvy (sacituzumab govitecan), an antibody drug conjugate ("ADC") approved to treat metastatic breast cancer, and granted accelerated approval to treat urothelial cancer; however, Gilead voluntarily withdrew the drug from this market after a follow-up study missed its primary endpoint.

The drug has failed to meet endpoints in two key studies in non-small cell lung cancer ("NSCLC"), and bladder cancer, and as other Pharmas bring more ADCs to market, Trodelvy's prospects seem to recede, although a recent win in triple negative breast cancer ("TNBC") offers some encouragement. On its Q3 earnings call with analysts, CEO O'Day told analysts:

Given the particularly aggressive nature of this disease, we are moving as quickly as we can to bring Trodelvy to first-line metastatic triple-negative breast cancer patients. We have submitted sBLAs with the FDA and are targeting a potential commercial launch in 2026 that could extend Trodelvy's leadership in breast cancer.

Kite Pharma, Gilead's Cell Therapy division - and a $12bn acquisition back in 2017 - has secured approval for Yescarta and Tecartus to treat heme cancers, and the two drugs earned $1.97bn in revenues in 2024, but year-to-date in 2025, only $1.38bn.

A new candidate, anito-cel, may be approved to treat multiple myeloma in 2026, Gilead says, although this is a fiercely contested market with several major Pharmas - Johnson & Johnson (JNJ) and Bristol Myers Squibb (BMY), for example, marketing and selling numerous products already.

Gilead once talked up the prospect of its oncology division accounting for ~30% of total revenues by 2030, but in 2025, I'd estimate the division will account for just 11% of revenues, and trodelvy, and an anti-TIGIT / anti-PD-1 partnership with Arcus Biosciences (RCUS) aside, the pipeline is more or less bare.

In December last year, Gilead announced that:

Dietmar Berger, MD, PhD, will join the company on January 2, 2025, as Chief Medical Officer and will become a member of Gilead’s senior leadership team. Dr. Berger succeeds Merdad Parsey, MD, PhD.

Most recently, Dr. Berger was the Chief Medical Officer and Global Head of Development at Sanofi, where he led development science, strategy and operations across the company’s therapeutic areas of focus. Prior to Sanofi, Dr. Berger served in senior development roles at Atara, Genentech, Bayer and Amgen.

The new CMO's background is primarily in oncology, and I would expect them to continue to prioritize the division and have funds to invest in much needed M&A, but on a smaller scale, and with greater scrutiny around R&D spending overall, I'd speculate.

That's the bad news, but otherwise, Gilead appears to be a flourishing company, led by its globally best-selling HIV division.

As we can see in the table above, since 2018, the HIV division has doubled in size, from $9.1bn per annum, to $19.6bn in 2024, and I have conservatively estimated $19.94bn for 2025. Sales in Q3 of $5.3bn were up 4% year-on-year, led by the "mega-blockbuster" Biktarvy, the world's fourth best-selling prescription drug in 2025, by my calculation. On its Q3 earnings call with analysts, Gilead noted:

Consistent with our performance year-to-date, we are increasing our guidance for full year HIV revenue growth to approximately 5%, up from 3% last quarter. Biktarvy sales of $3.7 billion, were up 6% year-over-year and 4% sequentially due to higher demand, reflecting continued market growth of 2% to 3% and strong commercial execution.

Biktarvy's year-over-year market share in the U.S. has grown every quarter since launch and achieved a record high of approximately 52% in the third quarter. Given Biktarvy's clear differentiation and market leadership, we're pleased that the expected loss of exclusivity in the U.S. for Biktarvy has been extended into 2036.

Apparently, Biktarvy will be the next of Gilead's drugs to lose its patent protection, which is highly encouraging, as once patents expire and generic drugs are permitted to enter the market, the original drug's revenues decline by 25% - 35% per annum owing to the extra competition, and competition on price.

Furthermore, management noted that "Descovy for PrEP (pre-exposure prophylaxis) achieved a new record market share of more than 45% in the U.S. in the third quarter. And finally, and perhaps most importantly, in June, Gilead launched Yeztugo, its long-acting HIV PrEP option.

Gilead has forecast $150m of revenues for the drug in 2025, but analysts believe peak annual revenues could one day fall between $4bn - $5bn - one analysts has suggested a figure as high as $8bn. The twice-yearly injectable is viewed as being more convenient that daily pills, including its own Truvada and Descovy products, and importantly, also more convenient than GSK's (GSK) Apretude (cabotegravir).

The drug will cost ~$28.2k list price (but may be available to patients at heavily discounted prices), and performed exceptionally well in clinical studies, with almost no infections reported by users. Gilead believes the drug can help increase the PrEP market in the U.S. from ~400k people, to >$1m people, and expects to enjoy a 60% market share in time. First, Yeztugo will have to show it can take market share away from cheaper generic pills, but the omens appear positive - management states that:

Yeztugo is increasingly recognized in clinical guidelines, including most recently the U.S. CDC. This strong endorsement of Yeztugo offers healthcare providers, public health leaders and communities clear guidance on an innovation that could help shift the trajectory of the HIV epidemic.

Looking Ahead: Can Anything Check Gilead's Momentum?

Gilead's liver disease division deserves an honorable mention, also - an overview is provided below:

I also include Veklury, the COVID therapy, in the above table to show how its revenues have declined, and will likely continue to do so over time.

Lizdelvi, approved to treat primary biliary cholangitis ("PBC") earlier this year, and part of Gilead's $4.3bn buyout of CymaBay last year, has proven a shrewd acquisition, with management telling analysts that the drug "exceeded $100 million in quarterly sales for the first time and is already the number 1 treatment for second-line PBC in the U.S." Analysts have suggested the drug could achieve peak revenues of ~$2bn per annum. "Other Products" will add ~$800m more in revenues to Gilead's top line in 2025, I'd estimate.

In terms of cash and debt, Gilead reported $8.49bn of near-term cash as of the end of Q3, current portion of long term as $(2.8bn), and long-term debt of $(22.1bn) - not onerous, by Big Pharma standards. Gilead paid $1bn in dividends in Q3 - current dividend yield is a respectable 2.5% - and repurchases $435m of shares, management says.

Arguably, Gilead's pipeline needs some work - which is probably why the previous Chief Medical Officer departed. The new CMO has an oncology background but, as mentioned above, won't want to repeat the same mistake of investing heavily in ultimately unsuccessful M&A deals. Instead, deals such as the Cyma Bay buyout, that bring in assets that are almost immediately earning commercial revenues, as opposed to more speculative assets still negotiating early-to-mid-stage clinical studies, may be a winning strategy, and ought to be accomplishable.

Ultimately, I feel as though in the past Gilead itself, and Wall Street analysts have arguably focused too much on what Gilead is not - an oncology / immunology powerhouse - and not enough on what it is - an HIV powerhouse with no major patent expiries due until 2036, according to the CEO.

Most of Gilead' efforts to expand in oncology, and immunology - e.g. Jyseleca and Cilofexor, targeting autoimmune disease and MASH - appear to have fallen by the wayside. Although the new CMO will likely attempt to build a respectable pipeline through strategic M&A and internal R&D, I can't see double-digit billion M&A deals being struck. With that said, Trodelvy has a shot of being a ~$3.5bn peak selling asset if its TNBC study data results in a label expansion.

I can see HIV being a >$25bn annual revenue division by 2030, and liver disease being a >$5bn division, and perhaps oncology too.

Gilead has shown no ambition to enter the GLP-1 receptor agonist T2D / obesity fields - Eli Lilly's stratospheric gains are built on its miracle drug tirzepatide, a GLP/GIP agonist, better known as Mounjaro / Wegovy, and set to become the world's best-selling drug this year - but I wonder if the company might look to do an M&A deal to gain access to a market that may suit it better than oncology immunology.

There are many companies - Structure Therapeutics (GPCR), Altimmune (ALT), Viking Therapeutics (VKTX), that could become a target, but ultimately, the bull thesis in relation to Gilead today is very much "if it ain't broke, don't try to fix it," in my view.

While a forward P/S of >5x is quite high for a Pharma giant, this is an immensely profitable company with a top-five globally revenue asset years away from patent expiry, supported by several more blockbuster or soon-to-be blockbuster HIV drugs, and a growing liver disease division.

There is money for M&A, and so long as Gilead doesn't throw that advantage away with poor deal-making, as it has done in the past, there is no reason why the company -which I estimate will be a $35bn per annum revenue company by 2020 - cannot maintain its P/S ratio of 5.5x, and be valued at a market cap nearer $200bn than $150bn by 2030. Long term, then, I see 15-20% more upside in only a modestly optimistic scenario.

data.title: JFrog: 'Sell' On Stretched Multiples (Downgrade)
data.description:
With very few exceptions, 2025 has been a tough year for many software companies, as the market's attention has shifted to AI and the potential disruptive impact that the foundational AI model companies may have on legacy enterprise software businesses. Pain has been especially concentrated in small- and mid-cap stocks, which are dealing with disruptive AI forces on top of macro-driven IT budget constraints.

JFrog (FROG), however, is one of the few exceptions. The company has delivered a series of beat-and-raise quarters this year that have made the stock very attractive as net retention rates improved. But we do have to take a step back and ask ourselves: After JFrog stock has doubled this year, does it have further left to rally?

I last wrote a "Neutral" article on JFrog in June, when the stock was trading at $50 per share. Previously, I had a "Buy" rating on JFrog all the way from January 2025 (when the stock was trading at a mere $31 per share) all the way through June, when I had called the stock a bona fide "growth at a reasonable price" stock. Having scored tremendous gains on my position now, I'm not hesitating to take profits on this trade and am downgrading JFrog to a "Sell" rating.

Since valuation is the principal reason that I am selling my position in JFrog (and the main reason I bought into the name when it was trading cheaply in the ~$30s), let's start first with a discussion of what I view to be JFrog's unsustainable multiples. At current share prices near $62, JFrog trades at a market cap of $7.33 billion. Netting off the $651.1 million of cash (against zero debt) on JFrog's latest balance sheet gives us an enterprise value of $6.68 billion.

Meanwhile, for the remainder of FY25, JFrog has upped its full-year revenue guidance to $523-$525 million in revenue, or 22%-23% y/y growth, up from a previous $507-$510 million (18%-19%) range. It's also now calling for a richer 16.7%-16.8% operating margin, roughly two points higher than the previous 14.8%-15.3%. Its 22% FCF margin implies $115.3 million in midpoint FCF.

For next year, FY26, Wall Street is expecting the company to deliver $611.2 million in revenue (+17% y/y). If we assume a 25% FCF margin against this revenue profile (midway between JFrog's FY25 margin guidance and its FY27 goal), FCF would be $152.8 million. This positions JFrog's valuation multiples at:

  • 12.7x EV/FY25 revenue and 10.9x EV/FY26 revenue
  • 58x EV/FY25 FCF and 44x EV/FY26 FCF

These are incredibly stretched valuation multiples that sit nowhere near peers. The chart below showcases forward revenue multiples for other enterprise software peers that have ~20% growth. Most have seen their multiples contract this year amid falling share prices. The most telling peer is direct peer GitLab (GTLB), which competes head-to-head against JFrog in the DevOps software space and yet trades at nearly half of its multiple:

At one point earlier this year, it was GitLab that commanded a premium over JFrog. Arguably, both stocks (as well as Atlassian (TEAM), in fact) suffer from the same macro risk: all of these platforms cater primarily to software developers, which is a career category at risk of being slimmed down by generative AI and vibe coding. We haven't seen this happen yet exactly, but fear of a reduction in paid seats has led to an erosion in share prices for GitLab and Atlassian, while JFrog has managed to stay afloat for unexplained reasons.

Make no mistake, I think JFrog is producing excellent results. At the same time, however, I don't see an opportunity for the company to see a continued appreciation in valuation multiples, which is what drove the majority of its gains this year as the company expanded from a mid-single-digit revenue multiple to a mid-teens multiple that now towers over peers. To me, this overvaluation carries tremendous risk of correction as we enter into a risk-off attitude in the stock market.

Lock in gains on this trade and reinvest the proceeds elsewhere.

Q3 download

Let's now go through GitLab's latest quarterly results in greater detail. The Q3 earnings summary is shown below:

JFrog's revenue grew 25% y/y to $136.9 million, beating Wall Street's expectations of $128.3 million (+18% y/y). Revenue growth accelerated slightly versus 23% y/y growth in Q2.

What investors are particularly cheering is JFrog's recent elevation in net revenue retention rates. Over the past two quarters, JFrog has delivered an impressive 118% net retention, which indicates that the average customer is expanding their spending on the JFrog platform by 18%.

This is a helpful metric since one of the major clouds hanging over the software industry is what impact AI will have on IT and software development departments and how it will impact paid seats in enterprise software tools. JFrog's net revenue expansion is a clear signal that no headcount contraction is happening just yet.

Management notes that healthy expansion is driven by larger customer commitments and robust consumption trends, reflecting the industry's race to build new applications to embed AI features, all of which are creating a shorter-term jump in developer needs, not the opposite. Per CFO Ed Grabscheid's remarks on the Q3 earnings call:

Our overachievement during the quarter was the result of strong go-to-market and operational execution, continued momentum in our cloud revenues, growing adoption in JFrog security products and expansion by customers within our enterprise-level subscriptions [...]

In Q3, 56% of total revenues came from Enterprise Plus subscriptions, up from 50% in the prior year, driven by ongoing execution of our enterprise go-to-market strategy and broader customer adoption of the JFrog platform, revenue contribution from Enterprise Plus subscriptions grew 39% year-over-year. Net dollar retention for the 4 trailing quarters was 118%, consistent with the prior quarter, highlighting the continued adoption of our security core products and increased cloud data consumption, resulting in higher customer commitments. We continue to demonstrate that our customers view JFrog solutions as mission-critical to their software supply chain with gross retention that equaled 97% as of the third quarter 2025.

That said, we do note as well that direct competitor GitLab is also growing at a high 20%s pace and yet is seemingly unable to get positive market attention for its results.

On the profitability front, we note that JFrog's pro forma operating margins jumped 520bps y/y to 18.7%:

JFrog remains a healthy "Rule of 40" software business, though the company's rich valuation multiples already reward the company for its strong execution.

Key takeaways

In my view, the only way to be successful in today's stock market is to be open to constantly readjusting our portfolios and not getting too attached to the winners in our portfolios. JFrog, in my view, is ripe for profit-taking amid a correction in most other mid-cap enterprise software names. "Sell" this stock and lock in gains.

data.title: ADMA Biologics: Why The Dip To $16 Resets The Buy Case
data.description:
Introduction

My last look at ADMA Biologics (ADMA) followed its Q4 2024 earnings report in March.

For some background, ADMA is up 159% since my original Buy rating in April 2024. The rally has been fueled by Asceniv’s outperformance in the primary humoral immunodeficiency, or PI, market. Asceniv has procured robust revenue growth (59% Y/Y in Q4 2024) and cash generation for the plasma-derived biologics developer.

However, upon conducting a DCF model, I ascertained in March that ADMA’s valuation baked in high expectations already. With my “base case” expectations of $500 million in 2025 revenue, 16% CAGR, and 25% FCF margin, I calculated that ADMA was overvalued by 17% (DCF value per share: $16.13).

Since my March analysis, at writing, ADMA has fallen ~17% compared to index (SPY) gains of ~19%, and now trades at ≈$16.40/share.

Earlier this month, ADMA reported its Q3 2025 earnings. A closer look supports ongoing bullishness in its stock despite challenging 2025 comps.

Q3 Earnings & Outlook

Now that ADMA’s revenue comps have matured, revenue growth has notably slowed. Q3 2025 revenue of $134.2 million represents just a 12% Y/Y increase, which comes materially below my March “base case.” That said, ADMA continues to make progress in profitability. Adjusted EBITDA grew 29% Y/Y to $58.7 million (43.7% adjusted EBITDA margin).

Looking ahead to 2026, ADMA projects “at least $630 million” in revenue, compared to “at least $510 million” in 2025, representing 23.5% Y/Y growth. Meanwhile, the adjusted EBITDA margin for full-year 2025 is projected at 46%. For 2026, the adjusted EBITDA margin was guided at ~56%. ADMA recently received “lot release authorization” from the FDA for its “yield-enhanced production,” which it believes will increase production yields by approximately 20%.

ADMA’s management put their “binoculars” on and guided FY29 revenue to reach “$1.1 billion or more, with anticipated outsized earnings growth and operational momentum from current margin levels.” Recall that my March DCF model projected revenue of $905 million and FCF of $226 million in “Year 5” (2029). This implies that ADMA can beat my “base case” CAGR of 16%, at least in the short term. My 16% CAGR is a nine-year projection, so I wouldn’t make any adjustments to my static assumption, as ADMA’s growth is likely to regress past 2029. Moreover, the growth is largely dependent on just one product (Asceniv), so getting to $1.1 billion in 2029 will require flawless execution (e.g., yield-enhanced process, continued market share gains in IG).

ADMA also expects that its working capital will normalize. Facility expansions have limited cash generation in the trailing twelve months, coming in at less than 10%. So, this should materially improve in the future. That said, ADMA’s limited FCF generation, for now, remains a feature of a “build-out” mode. Looking ahead, should management meet their expectations (e.g., over 40% net margin) while capex steps down and working capital normalizes, my 25% FCF margin estimate stands firmly. In fact, I think ADMA could outpace this figure.

Taking a closer look at 1H25 results: revenue was $236.8 million with a gross margin of 54.2%, but operating cash flow was only $1.5 million due to heavy builds in receivables (~$60 million) and inventory (~$21 million). Again, assuming some normalization, that allows a clear path for over 25% in 2026 and beyond. To play devil’s advocate, should its plasma/IG model reveal a structural working-capital drag, FCF margins could settle more in the high teens/low 20s. That’s why I provide a sensitivity analysis to account for such scenarios!

Valuation Assumptions

“Year 1 Revenue” now represents management expectations for 2026. I adjusted “Fraction of Year Elapsed” to -0.25 to “tell” the model that we are still one quarter away from 2026 so that it properly discounts future cash flows. My estimates of 16% CAGR and 25% FCF margin are unchanged. These are averages over the nine-year projection.

Most everything else is standard DCF methodology (e.g., “Risk-Free Rate” is 10Y Treasury) or was derived from ADMA’s latest financial statement.

So, since I’ve extended the model one year out (compared to my analysis in March), it is more favorable towards ADMA (projected DCF value per share of $18.11). Of course, it helps that ADMA is also cheaper since then, and Year 1 revenue is materially higher.

FY29 is now “Year 4,” which projects almost $1 billion in revenue. So, management expectations are in line with my CAGR estimate.

I suspect that the majority of uncertainty surrounds long-term growth and short-term margin performance.

As implied by the above Sensitivity Analysis, even if ADMA’s growth falls closer to 10%, FCF margins over 30% could buoy its valuation. This gives its valuation flexibility and a few paths towards outperformance.

Regarding near-term growth prospects, ADMA’s yield-enhanced manufacturing effectively raises the revenue ceiling on the existing Asceniv/Bivigam franchise without the need for new products. Earlier this year, ADMA bought a $12.5 million, five-acre facility near its Boca Raton manufacturing campus to further boost expansion.

Looking beyond the early-2030s (much of ADMA’s terminal value), the company hopes to soon expand Asceniv’s label to pediatrics. It also recently disclosed SG-001, which is described as a preclinical hyperimmune globulin targeting Streptococcus pneumoniae.

Reading Between The Lines

During the call, management cautioned about the broad adoption of Asceniv, stating, “We still stand firm on our position that Asceniv should not be used as a first-line therapy.” In my experience, it is rare for biopharma executives to call out something like this because first-line utilization drives volume (and, thus, revenues). The company is clearly more focused on leaning into a niche, complex-PI position to defend premium pricing (supporting margins).

Management called out the FDA lot release as “a major inflection point” that will drive sustained gross margin expansion and “accelerate year-over-year growth rates in 2026.”

Regarding SG-001, management explicitly stated that its $1.1 billion-plus revenue projection for FY29 does not include SG-001, despite approval expected before then. “We believe SG-001 represents a potential $300 million to $500 million in annual high-margin revenue opportunity with IP protection through at least 2037.” In this view, SG-001 is seen as a long-dated “call opinion” that is not needed to hit the $1.1 billion FY29 projection.

In any event, the core risk/reward in ADMA clearly hinges on Asceniv mix, plasma/yield execution, and payer economics. While Asceniv remains unique as an IVIG blended from normal and RSV-high-titer plasma, Ig replacement is highly individualized with many products. So, there is a material risk that Asceniv’s niche positioning can itself cap growth.

Final Words

Overall, 2026 is setting up to be a plentiful year for ADMA that should feature accelerating growth and margin expansion. The company clearly has a good amount of leverage on its side with Asceniv and its niche, but a materially large and premium position in the IVIG market.

Q3 and the updated outlook mostly validate my original framework (maintain “Buy”). ADMA is still in the early innings of a high-margin, cash-generative Asceniv franchise with several ways to win (e.g., yield, mix, SG-001), but a concentrated set of risks (e.g., single-product dependency, payer dynamics, prescribing habits) should cap investor allocation.

At ~$16/share, the stock no longer screens as "overvalued" on my updated DCF, meriting some adds ahead of the New Year.
